evidence: Evo-Memory's central contribution is to operationalize self-evolving memory: agents that retrieve, integrate, and update memory continuously during deployment under a unified search-synthesis-evolve loop.

Paper frames its evaluation as 'test-time evolution', a memory-level instantiation of test-time learning, and reviews the test-time-adaptation lineage (TENT, MEMO, T3A, TTT++) explicitly in the related-work section.
ExpRAG, a deliberately simple task-level baseline, outperforms hierarchical/workflow/dynamic-cheatsheet baselines on multi-turn AgentBoard tasks across both Gemini-2.5 and Claude backbones.
Pearson r=0.717 (Gemini-2.5 Flash) and r=0.563 (Claude 3.7 Sonnet) between ReMem gain over history baseline and within-dataset task-embedding coherence.
Paper formalises agent search as retrieval+reranking under execution-grounded relevance and constructs a 9,759-agent benchmark to instantiate the concept (Sections 1, 3, 4).

Empirical results across 2,952 task queries and 259 task descriptions show description-based retrievers/rerankers fall short of oracle execution-grounded ranking; lightweight probing partly closes the gap (Tables 2, 3, Figure 4).
FLEX defines and operationalizes the experience library as the core learnable artifact.
FLEX names and formalizes the forward learning from experience paradigm.
FLEX experimentally introduces and demonstrates plug-and-play experience-library inheritance across heterogeneous LLM agents.
GSM8k 5-epoch sweep shows power-law training accuracy scaling with library size and logistic-like library growth.
FLEX delivers +10 to +23 absolute gains on AIME25, +7 to +10 on USPTO50k, +8.9 to +13.7 on ProteinGym with no parameter updates.

Section 3 / Figure 2 explicitly frame EvolveR as a closed-loop experience lifecycle of offline self-distillation, online interaction with experience retrieval, and policy evolution via GRPO; the four-paradigm taxonomy in the teaser figure positions this lifecycle as the paper's central novel paradigm.

Ablation Table 2 shows EvolveR (self-distill) beats EvolveR (teacher-distill, GPT-4o-mini) at Qwen2.5-3B (0.382 vs 0.370 average EM) while the teacher wins at 1.5B (0.290 vs 0.270) and 0.5B (0.220 vs 0.150), establishing a capacity-dependent crossover within one controlled comparison.
Paper proposes and names the deep-and-wide information-seeking task as its central contribution and operationalizes it via the DeepWideSearch benchmark.
Average Success Rate of 2.39% across 18+ frontier LLM and agent-framework configurations on the DeepWideSearch benchmark; agents fail to improve width metrics over their LLM backbones.
Paper formalizes the agentic-skill four-tuple S=(C,pi,T,R) in section 'What Is an Agentic Skill?' and argues for it as a minimal schema.
Section 'Skill Lifecycle Model' organizes the literature around a seven-stage lifecycle (discovery to evaluation/update) with feedback loops.
Section 'Security, Trust, and Governance of Skills' enumerates threat categories, the trust-tier model, and the ClawHavoc anchor case study quantifying 1,184 malicious skills and 36.8% flaw rate.
Reports SkillsBench results: +16.2 pp avg with curated skills, -1.3 pp with self-generated; only 1 of 5 self-generation configs improved.

ClawHavoc case study quantifies 1,184 malicious skills, 36.8% flaw rate, 91% prompt-injection payloads, and 135,000+ exposed OpenClaw instances; argues binary scanners under-cover the (C,pi,T,R) audit surface.
Section 3 (Trajectory-Grounded Blame Attribution): the paper formalizes the Blamer LLM that converts trajectory diagnostics into module-wise blame scores b_pi(e) and selects argmax for mutation.
Section 2 (Preliminaries: Modularized Tool-Use Policy): defines the four-module composition Pi = pi_syn o pi_call o pi_sel o pi_plan with frozen base weights and evolvable specifications Theta.
Section 3 (Diversity-Aware Population Selection): introduces instance-wins selection with winner-frequency parent sampling on a held-out S_sel.

Blame-targeting ablation (Qwen3-8B): random=39.5, single-module variants 46-50.6, monolithic=49.1, EvoTool=57.0 average across four benchmarks. Main results table shows monotonic learning curves and >5-point gains over strongest baselines on both backbones.

Population-selection ablation (Qwen3-8B): static=48.6, greedy=52.0, top-k=52.7, instance-wins (EvoTool)=57.0 average; largest gains on heterogeneous benchmarks (tau-Bench +3.7 over top-k, BFCL +2.1 over top-k).
MUSE explicitly proposes the hierarchical (strategic/procedural/tool) Memory Module as its core contribution and names it as a novel architecture for experience-driven self-evolution.

MUSE achieves SOTA 51.78% on TAC (+20% relative); continuous-learning shows monotonic improvement across 3 iterations; zero-shot generalization to T_hard (23.65% -> 33.41% with memory); DeepSeek-V3 transfer (28.01% -> 36.75%).
Survey names and defines the MOP->MOA->MAO->MASE trajectory and articulates MASE as the lifelong, self-evolving stage.
Survey introduces the four-component (System Inputs, Agent System, Environment, Optimiser) feedback-loop framework as its central conceptual contribution.
Survey proposes Endure / Excel / Evolve as guiding principles for safe self-evolution, by analogy with Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics.

Survey cites Pan et al. 2025 finding that single LLMs with well-crafted prompts match complex multi-agent debate frameworks on multiple reasoning benchmarks, using this as motivation to move from handcrafted to self-evolving multi-agent systems.
Survey formalizes the operational definition of self-evolving agents and the POMDP-based framework.
Survey introduces 'misevolution' framing and the related Alignment Tipping Process (ATP) as emergent self-evolution-specific risks.
Paper organizes the field around the what/when/how/where decomposition; this is the survey's primary conceptual contribution.
Survey explicitly motivates self-evolving agents by arguing that static LLM agents are a critical bottleneck in open-ended interactive deployments.
Survey synthesizes misevolution, ATP, memory-driven reward hacking, and tool-vulnerability findings, arguing these failure modes are specific to self-evolving systems.
Implements episodic+semantic memory split as the core adaptation mechanism, with no parameter updates; directly instantiates self-evolving memory architecture.
Builds a two-tier experience library: episodic critiques stored per instance and semantic summary distilled across all training critiques.
Adaptation occurs entirely at inference time via retrieved memory; no gradient updates used. Introduces suggestibility to predict when test-time learning succeeds.
Learns from labeled examples forwardly by generating and storing natural-language critiques, then retrieving them at inference — no backpropagation through the policy model.
Introduces and defines the suggestibility metric to measure a model's receptivity to critique-encoded external reasoning in context.
EP+SEM_CRIT yields +8.1pp over zero-shot and +4.6pp over RAG across 6 datasets/7 models; critique grounding is the key differentiator over raw-label storage.
SkillOS proposes the trainable skill curator as a dedicated modular policy for managing the SkillRepo via RL, separate from the frozen executor.
SkillOS instantiates stages 2–7 of the skill lifecycle with a learned RL curation policy; the curator/executor split operationalises the distillation→storage→retrieval→execution→evaluation chain.
SkillRepo skills are agentic skills in Markdown format (YAML frontmatter as applicability condition, NL instructions as executable policy); the executor retrieves and applies them via BM25.
SkillRepo functions as an experience library for procedural skills; SkillOS adds a trainable curator to manage it rather than relying on heuristic operations.

SkillOS fits the experience-driven lifecycle: trajectories are distilled into skills by the curator, skills are stored and retrieved for future tasks, and the curator is updated via RL on downstream performance. It differs in decoupling the curation policy (trainable) from the execution policy (frozen).
SkillOS builds on the self-evolving agent taxonomy from this survey, specifically instantiating the MASE paradigm (Multi-Agent Self-Evolving) with a two-component modular design.
SkillOS is evaluated on streaming benchmark settings; Evo-Memory is cited as a related benchmark and baseline for agent memory evaluation.
Empirical results show SkillOS 8B RL-trained curator achieves SR=61.2 vs ~57 for Gemini-2.5-Pro zero-shot curator on ALFWorld with Qwen3-8B executor.
Skill-R1 operates directly on agentic skills as optimization targets — the skill generator produces natural-language skill procedures that steer a frozen task LLM
Skill-R1 introduces the recurrent skill evolution mechanism with bi-level GRPO and multi-generation rollout loops
Skill-R1 adds an RL-driven recurrent evolution stage to the skill lifecycle's refinement and evaluation phases
Skill-R1 trains only the skill generator via rollout experience; the task LLM parameters are never updated — fitting the forward-learning-from-experience paradigm
Both papers train RL-based skill improvement components for LLM agents without updating the task model; SkillOS trains a skill curator, Skill-R1 trains a skill generator/editor
Skill-R1 is cited in the related work as part of the agent skill landscape that SoK surveys; it extends the skill governance and lifecycle framing with RL-based recurrent evolution
Both use RL-based iterative optimization for skill/tool-use policy components while keeping the task LLM frozen; EvoTool uses blame attribution and evolutionary search, Skill-R1 uses bi-level GRPO
Skill-R1 GRPO outperforms Vanilla GRPO by +12.1pp on GAIA and +4.0pp on WebWalker; ablations isolate the bi-level objective as the key factor beyond rollout structure
SkillGen advances the distillation and evaluation/update stages of the skill lifecycle through contrastive induction and intervention-based verification
SkillGen introduces contrastive induction as a method for extracting skill diagnostics from mixed success/failure trajectory pools
SkillGen introduces skill intervention verification — measuring the causal net effect (repairs minus regressions) of a generated skill before deployment
SkillGen extends trajectory-based credit attribution to the full skill level: where EvoTool blames individual modules, SkillGen measures the net causal effect of an entire skill intervention
SkillGen addresses skill generation quality via contrastive induction; SkillOS addresses skill curation (when to update/delete) via RL training — complementary stages of the skill lifecycle

Both papers address automated agent skill improvement without retraining: Skill-R1 via RL-driven iterative revision of existing skills; SkillGen via contrastive induction and verification of newly synthesized skills
SkillGen implements the distillation and evaluation/update stages of the SoK skill lifecycle and instantiates Pattern-4 (self-evolving skill library) with empirical verification

Both use trajectory-based evidence to guide skill/policy refinement; EvoTool uses blame attribution at module level, SkillGen uses contrastive induction at skill level with causal net-effect verification

Both address inference-time agent self-improvement from trajectory experience without parameter updates; EVOLVER uses RL to distill principles, SkillGen uses contrastive induction to synthesize skills
SkillGen ablation A4 (no Failure Lessons) shows reduced gains; full system with contrastive induction achieves best result on every dataset-model pair

SkillGraph introduces the graph-structured skill library: skills as nodes in a directed graph with typed prerequisite/enhancement/co-occurrence edges, enabling dependency-aware retrieval and structured evolution.

SkillGraph implements and extends the retrieval/composition and evaluation/update stages of the skill lifecycle with graph topology — adding prerequisite/enhancement/co-occurrence edges, topological sorting, and co-evolution with RL policy.

SkillGraph's graph evolution operations (insert, merge, split, deprecate, edge reinforcement) constitute a structural skill curator that uses graph topology signals rather than a separate trainable policy.

SkillGraph ablation of graph-aware retrieval (-31.2 ALFWorld) and graph structure (-11.7 WebShop) directly supports the claim that graph-structured retrieval outperforms flat libraries on compositional tasks.

SkillGraph extends EvolveR's experience-driven self-evolution paradigm by organizing distilled skills into a graph rather than a flat experience base, and cites EvolveR (wu2025evolver) as a memory-augmented RL baseline.
